title: Platner Drops His Senate Bid, and Who Should Replace Him
body:
It is official. Graham Platner has dropped his bid for U.S. Senate after his former partner, Jenny Racicot, accused him of rape, an allegation I find credible and abhorrent. He denies it. His exit comes just before the deadline that lets the Maine Democratic Party select his replacement to take on Susan Collins.

A personal note: I volunteered on Platner's campaign from the beginning, but this accusation forced me to leave immediately. I have a zero-tolerance policy for behavior of this kind, and I support his decision to drop out.

So where does that leave Maine? Thousands of people were energized by a populist, anti-billionaire, working-class message. They are now betrayed and disappointed, with nowhere to put that energy and no candidate in a race against a senator who has repeatedly outrun her polls and outlasted scandal.

Platner beat Janet Mills in a landslide because Maine made a clear choice: not an establishment Democrat, but a progressive outsider. The replacement who best matches that is former Maine Senate President Troy Jackson, a working-class progressive from a family of northern Maine loggers, with the experience Platner lacked and real name recognition. Bellows or Wood could carry the energy too, but Jackson strikes me as the most logical pick to harness it.

I break down the full field over on Food and Politics.

links.label: Read the full piece on Food & Politics

title: Susan Collins Faces Scrutiny Over ICE Shooting in Maine
body:
Senator Collins is under scrutiny this week after ICE agents shot and killed Johan Sebastian Duran Guerrero in Biddeford. Reporting indicates he was legally authorized to work in the US and was not the initial target of the surveillance operation. He was a well-known member of his community, survived by his wife and young daughter.

The shooting, the second ICE-related killing this week, sparked large protests at the Scarborough detention center, with ire directed at both ICE and Collins. She has spent months touting her efforts to scale back ICE activity in Maine, but that sits uneasily beside her vote to fund the agency after Democrats shut it down from February to April demanding policy changes. Her office is now calling for an end to non-essential ICE activity in the state, and the administration says it is halting vehicle stops.

For the Senate race, the lesson of the week is that the energy Graham Platner harnessed is still very much present in Maine, even if the movement is currently without a head. These protests will factor into the Democratic Party's choice of his successor later this month, and could hurt Collins in future polling once a nominee is selected to face her.

links.label: Read the full piece on Food & Politics

title: Dan Osborn, Nebraska's Independent Candidate
body:
Over in Nebraska, an independent with no major-party ties keeps making waves in this November's Senate race. Dan Osborn is a union leader, industrial mechanic, and Navy veteran who rose to prominence leading the 2021 Kellogg's strike in Omaha, was fired shortly after (he says in retaliation), and ran as an independent against Senator Deb Fischer in 2024. In deep-red Nebraska he polled shockingly close, some surveys even had it tied, before Fischer held on. Now he is back, running against the state's other Republican incumbent, Pete Ricketts.

Osborn's campaign is meaningful for the same reason Platner's was in Maine: both turned working-class disillusionment into a statewide movement against a powerful incumbent. I want to be clear that I am comparing the movements, not the men or their character. There is also an unconfirmed accusation floating around that Osborn recruited Platner to run in Maine, a piece of drama still playing out that I will keep watching. What the two share is a populist, anti-billionaire, pro-worker message that lands in places the political establishment left behind.

Nebraska is one of those places. Its economy leans heavily on agriculture, an industry gutted over decades by consolidation as large conglomerates buy up small farms and control prices, and now squeezed further by Trump-era tariffs cutting the value of exported goods. That financial pressure has bred a deep frustration, and Osborn has managed to harness it.

On The Needle Project, I built this race factoring in Nebraska's strong Republican lean and Osborn's 2024 performance, and right now Ricketts holds the edge. But that does not yet factor in polling, because the only substantial polls so far were funded by the Osborn camp and have been questioned on methodology. Quality nonpartisan polling is still missing. The Democratic nominee, Cindy Burbank, has dropped out to back Osborn. My read: Osborn has tapped into something real in rural America, anti-billionaire and pro-worker candidates have done well in 2026, and once quality polls land I expect them to show a tighter race than the fundamentals suggest, close all the way to November.

title: Jackson Holds the Delegate Advantage Ahead of the July 23 Debate
body:
A quick note on the Maine Senate replacement race: a vast majority of the chosen Democratic delegates who will select Maine's replacement nominee were supportive of Troy Jackson's bid, giving him a heavy advantage heading into the upcoming debate between the major candidates on July 23 on CNN.

The party must certify its new nominee by July 27, so the debate is effectively the field's last big chance to move delegates before the vote. Once the nominee is set, the Maine Senate needle comes back online with the new matchup.

title: A Nominee? Before Nomination Day?
body:
Yes and no. Technically, Troy Jackson hasn't officially been picked as the party nominee, but the title isn't clickbait: he is definitely the presumptive nominee, and something strange would need to happen for that to change in five days.

Here is the process. After Graham Platner dropped his bid, the Maine Democratic Party had each county's Democrats meet to elect delegates, who will attend a convention on July 25 and vote on the next nominee for Senate. They have not voted yet, but we already have plenty of clues about who they will pick, which is what led the major contenders to drop out. This past weekend, 500 of the 601 delegate slots were selected across the state. Of those 500, more than 460 confirmed they will support Troy Jackson, giving him a commanding portion of the convention before it even meets.

After that, nearly all of the other Democrats dropped out. Nirav Shah ended his campaign Sunday evening and endorsed Jackson, along with Jordan Wood and Dan Kleban. Shenna Bellows also ended her bid, and Paige Loud exited earlier in the weekend. At this point it is a foregone conclusion: Troy Jackson is the new Democratic nominee for Senate, and he will face Susan Collins in November.

Who is he? Born in Fort Kent and raised in Allagash, Jackson served in the Maine State Senate (2008-2014 and 2016-2024), including as Senate president and majority leader, representing northern Aroostook County. His career is a study in evolution: he first ran as a Republican, was first elected as an Independent, and joined the Democratic Party in 2004. Once a social conservative, he shifted to earn 100% scorecards from the Planned Parenthood Action Fund and EqualityMaine. His platform has always centered on health care and workers' rights, and in 2016 he used his superdelegate vote for Bernie Sanders over Hillary Clinton. He has drawn endorsements from Ro Khanna, the Maine DSA, and Hasan Piker.

His platform is pro-worker and anti-billionaire, aimed at making Maine affordable for working people: codifying abortion rights federally, Medicare for All, banning conversion therapy, and stronger anti-discrimination law. He supports aid to Ukraine and opposes an Iran war. He backs fully abolishing and dismantling ICE, has called Israel's campaign in Gaza a genocide, and says he would not support additional military aid to Israel.

What does it mean? Jackson can rise to the movement Platner built before his implosion, riding the same upswell of disillusionment and discontent with billionaires, the Israeli government, and rising costs. He is not the best public speaker in the world, but he offers the same platform with deep experience in the state, a track record of listening to constituents, and a real base among rural, working-class Mainers. He has been at this movement far longer than Platner and is a well-known name. Polling has put Jackson and Collins in a dead heat, and that will be worth watching all the way to November.
